The Widget class manages the rendering of dynamic sidebar content (widgets). It supports both built-in system widgets and custom widgets provided by modules.

docs.title: Show Method
docs.text:
Usage: echo Widget::show(string $location = 'sidebar');

Fetches all active widgets assigned to a specific location and returns the generated HTML. - $location: The target position (e.g., 'sidebar', 'footer-1'). - The order is determined by the sorting value in the widgets table.

// In a theme file (e.g., sidebar.php)
echo Widget::show('sidebar');

docs.title: Supported Widget Types
docs.text:
The content() method automatically handles various widget types:

  1. Recent Posts: Displays the latest 5 posts with thumbnails.
  2. Recent Comments: Fetches the newest reader comments.
  3. Tag Cloud: Generates a cloud of frequently used tags.
  4. Custom Module: Triggers a hook (specified in the content field) to load dynamic content from a third-party module.
  5. Static Content: Returns raw HTML or text entered manually in the admin area.

docs.title: UI Structure
docs.text:
Each widget is wrapped in a standard container for easy styling:

<div class="widget-box mb-4" id="widget-ID">
    <div class="widget-header">
        <h3 class="widget-title h5 fw-bold m-0">Widget Title</h3>
    </div>
    <div class="widget-body">
        Widget Content...
    </div>
</div>

docs.title: Management
docs.text: Widgets are managed in the admin dashboard under Appearance > Widgets, where users can drag and drop to reorder, edit titles, and assign them to specific theme locations.

docs.title: Xaptcha Class
docs.text:
# filename: Xaptcha.class.php
# location: inc/lib/
# package: GeniXCMS
# since: 0.0.4-patch
# author: Puguh Wijayanto (metalgenix.id)
# copyright: 2014-2024 Puguh Wijayanto
# license: MIT License

Info

This Documentation is still need improvement.

The Xaptcha class provides a simple wrapper for Google reCAPTCHA v2, allowing for easy implementation of bot protection in forms like comments, registration, and login.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: CAPTCHA settings are managed via the system dashboard under Settings > General: - google_captcha_enable: Set to 'on' to enable. - google_captcha_sitekey: Your public site key from Google. - google_captcha_secret: Your private secret key for server-side verification. - google_captcha_lang: Language code for the widget (e.g., 'en', 'id').

docs.title: HTML Method
docs.text:
Usage: echo Xaptcha::html();

Generates and returns the reCAPTCHA widget HTML along with the necessary JavaScript library from Google's CDN. It only returns content if CAPTCHA is enabled.

docs.title: Verify Method
docs.text:
Usage: if (Xaptcha::verify($_POST['g-recaptcha-response'])) { ... }

Performs server-side verification of the CAPTCHA response. - $gresponse: The token submitted by the form ($_POST['g-recaptcha-response']). - Uses the official ReCaptcha PHP library (autoloaded via Vendor). - Returns true if Google confirms the response as valid.

docs.title: IsEnable Method
docs.text:
Usage: Xaptcha::isEnable();

Returns true if the CAPTCHA system is globally enabled in the site options.

docs.title: Implementation Example
docs.text:
// In your form template
if (Xaptcha::isEnable()) {
    echo Xaptcha::html();
}

// In your processing controller
if (Xaptcha::isEnable()) {
    if (!isset($_POST['g-recaptcha-response']) || !Xaptcha::verify($_POST['g-recaptcha-response'])) {
        die("Captcha verification failed!");
    }
}

docs.title: How to Create a Module
docs.text: This guide walks you through building a GeniXCMS module from scratch, using a practical "Contact Form" example.

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
A module lives in inc/mod/{module-slug}/ and must contain at minimum:

inc/mod/my-module/
├── index.php           ← Required: Module entry point + metadata
├── options.php         ← Optional: Admin settings page
├── inc/
│   └── MyModule.lib.php  ← Main module class
└── layout/
    └── myview.php      ← Frontend template (Latte)

docs.title: Step 1: Create index.php (Entry Point)
docs.text:
The comment block at the top is parsed by the CMS to display the module in the admin panel.

<?php
/**
 * Name: My Module
 * Desc: A brief description of what this module does.
 * Version: 1.0.0
 * Build: 1.0.0
 * Developer: Your Name
 * URI: https://yoursite.com
 * License: MIT License
 * Icon: fa fa-cogs
 */

// Register a PSR-style autoloader for the module's own classes
function loadMyModuleLib($class_name)
{
    Mod::inc($class_name . ".lib", '', dirname(__FILE__) . "/inc/");
}
spl_autoload_register('loadMyModuleLib');

// Instantiate the main module class
new MyModule();

docs.title: Step 2: Create the Module Class (inc/MyModule.lib.php)
docs.text:
The module class constructor hooks into the CMS. The mod_control hook is the key action point for frontend pages.

<?php
class MyModule
{
    public function __construct()
    {
        // Attach to the frontend page hook
        Hooks::attach('mod_control', ['MyModule', 'myPage']);

        // Register this module's page in the Menus admin dropdown
        Mod::addMenuList(['myPage' => 'My Module Page']);

        // Optional: Attach to other hooks
        Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['MyModule', 'loadAssets']);
        Hooks::attach('admin_page_dashboard_action', ['MyModule', 'dashboardWidget']);
    }

    /**
     * Frontend page handler.
     * $data[0]['mod'] contains the module name from the URL.
     */
    public static function myPage($data)
    {
        // Only execute when this module's URL is active
        if ($data[0]['mod'] !== 'myPage') {
            return;
        }

        $page_data = [];

        // Handle form submission
        if (isset($_POST['submit'])) {
            $token = Typo::cleanX($_POST['token']);
            if (!Token::validate($token)) {
                $page_data['alertDanger'][] = _('Invalid security token.');
            } else {
                $message = Typo::cleanX($_POST['message']);
                // Process the form...
                $page_data['alertSuccess'][] = _('Submitted successfully!');
            }
        }

        // Render a view from the layout folder
        Mod::inc('myview', $page_data, realpath(__DIR__ . '/../layout/'));
    }

    public static function loadAssets()
    {
        echo '<script src="' . Site::$url . '/inc/mod/my-module/assets/app.js"></script>';
    }

    public static function dashboardWidget($data)
    {
        // Output a widget on the admin dashboard
        echo '<div class="col-md-4"><div class="info-box">My Module Stats</div></div>';
    }
}

docs.title: Step 3: Create a Frontend View (layout/myview.php)
docs.text:
Layout files use the Latte template engine.

<section class="container my-5">
    <h1>My Module Page</h1>

    {* Display alerts *}
    {if isset($alertSuccess)}
        <div class="alert alert-success">{$alertSuccess[0]}</div>
    {/if}

    {* Main form *}
    <form method="POST" action="">
        <input type="hidden" name="token" value="{$token}">

        <div class="form-group">
            <label>Your Message</label>
            <textarea name="message" class="form-control" required></textarea>
        </div>

        <button type="submit" name="submit" class="btn btn-primary">Submit</button>
    </form>
</section>

docs.title: Step 4: Create options.php (Admin Settings)
docs.text:
The options.php file is shown in Admin > Modules > [Your Module] > Settings.

<?php
// Save options
if (isset($_POST['mymodule_save'])) {
    $data = [
        'notify_email' => Typo::cleanX($_POST['notify_email']),
    ];
    Options::update('mymodule_options', json_encode($data));
}

$opt = json_decode(Options::get('mymodule_options'), true) ?? [];
?>
<section class="content">
    <h1><i class="fa fa-cogs"></i> My Module Settings</h1>
    <div class="box">
        <div class="box-body">
            <form method="POST">
                <div class="form-group">
                    <label>Notification Email</label>
                    <input type="email" name="notify_email" class="form-control"
                           value="<?= $opt['notify_email'] ?? '' ?>">
                </div>
                <button type="submit" name="mymodule_save" class="btn btn-primary">Save</button>
            </form>
        </div>
    </div>
</section>

docs.title: How Modules Are Loaded
docs.text:
When a user visits /mod/myPage.html (with Smart URL) or ?mod=myPage:

  1. Control::frontend() detects the mod route.
  2. Control::incFront('mod') includes Frontend/mod.control.php.
  3. mod.control.php fires Hooks::run('mod_control', $param).
  4. Your module's myPage($data) function executes, checking $data[0]['mod'].
/mod/myPage.html
  → Control::incFront('mod', $param)
  → mod.control.php
  → Hooks::run('mod_control', $param)
  → MyModule::myPage($data)
  → Mod::inc('myview', $page_data, '.../layout/')

docs.title: Useful Mod Class Methods
docs.text:
// Include a view file from a custom path
Mod::inc('view-name', $data, '/path/to/layout/');

// Register module page in menu manager
Mod::addMenuList(['hookName' => 'Display Label']);

docs.title: Available Hooks for Modules
docs.text: Hook Type Description mod_control Action Frontend page execution point admin_page_dashboard_action Action Add widget to admin dashboard admin_page_top_action Action Output at top of admin page admin_page_bottom_action Action Output at bottom of admin page footer_load_lib Action Add JS/CSS to page footer header_load_meta Action Add tags to HTML <head> post_content_filter Filter Modify post content before display post_submit_add_action Action Fires after a new post is saved user_login_action Action Fires after successful user login

docs.title: Complete Example: Contact Form Module
docs.text:
The built-in Contact Form module demonstrates this full pattern:

inc/mod/contact-form/
├── index.php               — Registers autoloader, instantiates Contact class
├── options.php             — Admin instructions panel
├── inc/
│   └── Contact.lib.php     — Main class: hooks into mod_control,
│                             handles POST, sends email via Mail::send()
└── layout/
    └── contactpage.php     — Latte form template

Key integration points from Contact.lib.php: 

// In constructor:
Hooks::attach('mod_control', ['Contact', 'contactPage']);
Mod::addMenuList(['contactPage' => 'Contact Us']);

// In contactPage():
if ($data[0]['mod'] == 'contactPage') {
    // Handle POST, validate, call Mail::send()
    Mod::inc('contactpage', $data, realpath(__DIR__.'/../layout/'));
}

docs.title: Installing the Module
docs.text:
1. Place your module folder in inc/mod/my-module/.
  2. Go to Admin > Modules.
  3. Find your module and click Install.
  4. To add a frontend page link, go to Admin > Menus and create a new menu item selecting Mod type with your module's page name.

docs.title: 1. Database Migrations
docs.text: Don't use Db::query() in your constructor to create tables. Use the Migration System. 1. Create a migration file in inc/migrations/. 2. Use Db::query() within the migration's up() method to create your tables. 3. Users will run php gx-console migrate to setup your module's database.

docs.title: 2. Access Control List (ACL)
docs.text:
Register custom permissions for your module features in your constructor:

Acl::register('MOD_MYMODULE_CAN_EXPORT', 'Allow users to export my module data');

Then check it before any sensitive action: 

if (Acl::check('MOD_MYMODULE_CAN_EXPORT')) {
    // Perform export...
}

docs.title: 3. API Resources
docs.text: Expose your module data over the RESTful API: 1. Create inc/lib/Control/Api/MyResourceApi.class.php. 2. Your resource will be available at /api/v1/myresource/.

docs.title: Module Security (CSP & Assets)
docs.text: If your module injects scripts from external CDNs (like Google Maps or Chart.js), you must register these domains in the Content Security Policy whitelist.

docs.title: Registering Trusted Origins
docs.text:
Use the system_security_headers_args hook in your module's constructor:

// In inc/mod/my-module/inc/MyModule.lib.php
public function __construct() {
    Hooks::attach('system_security_headers_args', [$this, 'extendCSP']);
}

/**
 * Add trusted origins to the global CSP.
 */
public function extendCSP($rules) {
    if (!in_array("https://maps.googleapis.com", $rules['script-src'])) {
        $rules['script-src'][] = "https://maps.googleapis.com";
    }
    return $rules;
}

[!CAUTION] Avoid using http:// for external assets. The core security policy blocks insecure sources by default to prevent mixed content vulnerabilities.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hooks Class — All available hooks.
  • Mod Class — Module utilities.
  • Mail Class — Send emails from modules.
  • Options Class — Store and retrieve module settings.
  • Token Class — CSRF protection for module forms.
  • Control Layer Reference — How module routes are dispatched.
  • Model Class — Base ORM class.

docs.title: How to Create a Theme
docs.text: This guide walks you through creating a custom GeniXCMS theme from scratch.

docs.title: Theme Structure
docs.text:
A theme lives in inc/themes/{your-theme-name}/ and must contain at minimum:

inc/themes/my-theme/
├── themeinfo.php       ← Required: Theme metadata
├── header.php          ← Required: Page header (HTML head + navbar)
├── footer.php          ← Required: Page footer
├── index.php           ← Required: Homepage template
├── single.php          ← Required: Single post template
├── function.php        ← Optional: Theme class + hooks
└── options.php         ← Optional: Admin settings panel

Additional template files you can create:

File Used When page.php Content type is page cat.php Category archive listing tag.php Tag archive listing author.php Author archive listing archive.php Date-based archive search.php Search results 404.php Page not found

docs.title: Step 1: Create themeinfo.php
docs.text:
This file contains theme metadata. The comment block is parsed by the admin panel.

<?php
/*
 * Name: My Theme
 * Desc: A minimal theme for GeniXCMS
 * Version: 1.0.0
 * Build: 1.0.0
 * Developer: Your Name
 * URI: https://yoursite.com
 * License: MIT License
 * Icon: fa fa-paint-brush
 */

docs.title: Step 2: Create header.php
docs.text:
The header file is rendered using the Latte template engine. All variables from BaseControl::initCommonData() are automatically available.

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="{$website_lang}">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>{$sitetitle|noescape} - {$site_name}</title>
    {$site_meta|noescape}

    <link rel="stylesheet" href="{$theme_url}/assets/css/style.css">
</head>
<body>
<nav class="navbar">
    <a href="{$site_url}">{$site_logo|noescape}</a>
    {Menus::getMenu('main')|noescape}
</nav>

Important

Do not close </body> or </html> in the header. The footer file closes them.

docs.title: Available Global Variables in All Templates
docs.text: Variable Description $site_name Site title $site_url Base URL $site_logo Pre-rendered <img> logo tag $theme_url URL to your theme folder $token CSRF security token $website_lang 2-letter active language code $sitetitle Current page title $site_meta SEO meta tags (title, description, OG)

docs.title: Step 3: Create footer.php
docs.text:
<footer class="site-footer">
    <p>{$site_footer|noescape}</p>
</footer>
{Hooks::run('footer_load_lib')|noescape}
</body>
</html>

Tip

Always call {Hooks::run('footer_load_lib')|noescape} just before </body>. It loads all JavaScript libraries registered by the system and modules.

docs.title: Step 4: Create index.php (Homepage)
docs.text:
The homepage receives a $posts array (processed post objects) and pagination HTML.

<main class="container">
    {foreach $posts as $post}
        <article>
            <h2><a href="{Url::post($post->id)}">{$post->title|noescape}</a></h2>
            <p class="meta">
                By {$post->author} |
                {Date::format($post->date)|noescape} |
                {Categories::name($post->cat)}
            </p>
            <div class="excerpt">
                {Posts::excerpt($post->content, 200)|noescape}
            </div>
        </article>
    {/foreach}

    <div class="pagination">
        {$paging|noescape}
    </div>
</main>

docs.title: Step 5: Create single.php (Post Detail)
docs.text:
The single post template receives fully prepared post data.

<main class="container">
    <article>
        <h1>{$title|noescape}</h1>

        <div class="post-meta">
            By <a href="{$url_author}">{$author}</a> |
            {$date_published} |
            <a href="{$url_category}">{$category_name}</a>
        </div>

        <div class="post-content">
            {$content|noescape}
        </div>

        <div class="post-tags">
            {$post_tags|noescape}
        </div>
    </article>

    {* Comments Section *}
    {Comments::showList(['max' => 10])|noescape}
    {Comments::form()|noescape}
</main>

docs.title: Step 6: Create function.php (Optional)
docs.text:
The function.php file is automatically loaded when the theme is active. Use it to define a theme class that registers hooks and provides helper methods.

<?php
class MyTheme
{
    public static $opt;

    public function __construct()
    {
        // Load theme options from the database
        $opt_raw = Options::get('mytheme_options');
        self::$opt = json_decode($opt_raw, true) ?? [];

        // Register hooks
        Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['MyTheme', 'loadAssets']);
        Hooks::attach('header_load_meta', ['MyTheme', 'loadFonts']);
    }

    public static function opt($key)
    {
        return self::$opt[$key] ?? '';
    }

    public static function loadFonts()
    {
        echo '<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@400;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">';
    }

    public static function loadAssets()
    {
        echo '<script src="' . Url::theme() . '/assets/js/app.js"></script>';
    }
}

new MyTheme();

docs.title: Step 7: Create options.php (Admin Settings)
docs.text:
The options.php file is shown in the admin under Appearance > Themes > Settings. GeniXCMS 2.0.0+ provides the OptionsBuilder class to quickly create professional-grade settings panels.

<?php
// Handle form save
$saveKey = 'mytheme_options_save';
if (isset($_POST[$saveKey])) {
    unset($_POST[$saveKey]);
    $data = [];
    foreach ($_POST as $k => $v) { $data[$k] = $v; }

    Options::update('mytheme_options', json_encode($data));
    echo '<div class="alert alert-success">Settings saved!</div>';
}

$opt = json_decode(Options::get('mytheme_options'), true) ?? [];

// Define the settings schema
$schema = [
    [
        'id'       => 'tab-general',
        'label'    => 'General Settings',
        'icon'     => 'fa fa-cog',
        'group'    => 'General',
        'title'    => 'General Settings',
        'cards'    => [
            [
                'title'  => 'Hero Content',
                'fields' => [
                    ['type' => 'text',  'name' => 'hero_title', 'label' => 'Hero Title'],
                    ['type' => 'color', 'name' => 'primary_color', 'label' => 'Primary Color'],
                ]
            ],
            [
                'title'  => 'Typography',
                'fields' => [
                    ['type' => 'typo_row', 'prefix' => 'typo_body', 'label' => 'Body Text Font'],
                ]
            ]
        ]
    ]
];

$builder = new OptionsBuilder($opt, [], [], [
    'brandName' => 'My Theme',
    'saveKey'   => $saveKey
]);
$builder->render($schema);

docs.title: Latte Template Syntax Cheatsheet
docs.text: Task Syntax Print variable (escaped) {$variable} Print variable (raw HTML) {$variable\|noescape} Print PHP function result {ClassName::method($arg)\|noescape} If condition {if $condition} ... {/if} For each loop {foreach $items as $item} ... {/foreach} Define local variable {var $x = 'value'} Truncate string {$text\|truncate:200} Strip HTML tags {$text\|stripHtml}

docs.title: Using Hooks in Templates
docs.text:
{* Run an action hook (output) *}
{Hooks::run('my_custom_section', $data)|noescape}

{* Apply a filter hook *}
{Hooks::filter('post_title_filter', $title)|noescape}

docs.title: Activating the Theme
docs.text: Go to Admin > Appearance > Themes and click Activate next to your theme. The theme folder name becomes the active theme identifier stored in Options.

docs.title: Security & Content Security Policy (CSP)
docs.text: GeniXCMS 2.0.0+ enforces a strict Content Security Policy. If your theme loads external scripts, fonts, or styles from a CDN that is not in the default whitelist, you must register it in your function.php.

docs.title: How to Whitelist a Theme CDN
docs.text:
// In inc/themes/my-theme/function.php
Hooks::attach('system_security_headers_args', function($rules) {
    // Add external script source
    $rules['script-src'][] = "https://cdn.my-library.js";

    // Add external font source
    $rules['font-src'][] = "https://fonts.my-custom-provider.com";

    return $rules;
});

[!TIP] Always use HTTPS for your external assets. Insecure http:// sources are blocked by the system security policy to prevent mixed content issues.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Theme Class — Theme loading and asset management API.
  • Hooks Class — Available hooks for themes.
  • BaseControl Class — Global data available in all templates.
  • Url Class — URL generation helpers.

docs.title: How to Use the Internal Ajax API
docs.text:
GeniXCMS provides an internal JSON-based API accessible at /ajax/api/. This is primarily intended for internal frontend interactions (like search suggestions or theme widgets) and requires a valid CSRF session token.

[!IMPORTANT] For external applications, mobile apps, or headless CMS integrations, please use the RESTful API (v1) which uses secure API Keys and standard REST terminology.

docs.title: Base URL
docs.text:
{site_url}/ajax/api/{TOKEN}?action={action}

For non-Smart URL mode: 

{site_url}/index.php?ajax=api&token={TOKEN}&action={action}

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: The API uses CSRF tokens for protected endpoints. Public endpoints do not require a token.

docs.title: Getting a Token
docs.text:
The current session token is available as the TOKEN constant in templates:

{* In a Latte template *}
<script>
    const API_TOKEN = '{TOKEN}';
</script>

In PHP: 

$token = TOKEN; // Available globally after session is initialized

docs.title: recent_posts (Public)
docs.text:
Returns the most recent posts with metadata and thumbnail URLs.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=recent_posts&num=5&type=post

Parameters:

Parameter Type Default Description num int 5 Number of posts to return type string 'post' Content type: 'post' or 'page' cat int (none) Filter by category ID

Response: 

{
    "status": "success",
    "data": [
        {
            "id": 42,
            "title": "My Post Title",
            "url": "https://example.com/my-post.html",
            "date": "26 March 2026",
            "author": "admin",
            "author_url": "https://example.com/author/admin/",
            "category": "Technology",
            "category_url": "https://example.com/category/1/technology/",
            "image": "https://example.com/thumb/type/square/size/300/...",
            "excerpt": "A short preview of the post content..."
        }
    ]
}

docs.title: categories (Public)
docs.text:
Returns all categories of a given type.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=categories&type=post

Parameters:

Parameter Type Default Description type string 'post' Taxonomy type: 'post' or 'page'

Response: 

{
    "status": "success",
    "data": [
        { "id": 1, "name": "Technology", "slug": "technology" },
        { "id": 2, "name": "Travel", "slug": "travel" }
    ]
}

docs.title: authors (Public)
docs.text:
Returns a unique list of usernames who have published content.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=authors

Response: 

{
    "status": "success",
    "data": [
        { "author": "admin" },
        { "author": "p_wijayanto" }
    ]
}

docs.title: tags (Token Required)
docs.text:
Returns tag suggestions matching a search term. Used for autocomplete.

Request: 

GET /ajax/api/TOKEN?action=tags&term=php

Parameters:

Parameter Type Description term string Prefix to search for matching tag names

docs.title: JavaScript Usage Example
docs.text:
const SITE_URL = 'https://example.com';
const TOKEN = '...'; // from your Latte template

// Fetch recent posts
fetch(`${SITE_URL}/ajax/api/${TOKEN}?action=recent_posts&num=6`)
    .then(res => res.json())
    .then(({ status, data }) => {
        if (status !== 'success') return;
        data.forEach(post => {
            console.log(post.title, post.url);
        });
    });

docs.title: Error Responses
docs.text: Scenario Response Invalid or missing token (protected action) {"status":"error","message":"Token not exist or invalid"} Unknown action {"status":"error","message":"Unknown action"}

docs.title: Adding Custom API Actions
docs.text:
You can extend the API from a module by listening to the api-ajax controller. Create a custom Ajax controller at:

inc/mod/my-module/ajax/myaction-ajax.control.php

Or attach to an existing hook if available:

// In your module class constructor:
Hooks::attach('api_action_myaction', ['MyModule', 'handleApiAction']);

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Control Layer Reference — How AJAX routes are dispatched.
  • Token Class — How CSRF tokens are generated and validated.
  • Http Class — For making outbound HTTP requests from the server side.

docs.title: How to Use Hooks
docs.text:
Hooks are the primary extension mechanism in GeniXCMS. They allow themes and modules to add functionality or modify output without editing core files.

There are two types of hooks: - Actions — Execute a function at a specific point (no return value expected). - Filters — Modify a value and return the modified result.

docs.title: Attaching an Action
docs.text:
Usage: Hooks::attach(string $hook_name, callable $callback, int $priority = 10);

// Attach a function to the 'footer_load_lib' hook
Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['MyTheme', 'loadAssets']);

// Or with a closure
Hooks::attach('post_submit_add_action', function($data) {
    // Send a notification email when a post is added
    Mail::send([
        'to'      => 'admin@example.com',
        'subject' => 'New Post Published',
        'message' => 'A new post was just published.',
    ]);
});

docs.title: Running an Action
docs.text:
Usage: Hooks::run(string $hook_name, mixed $data = '');

// In a theme template (Latte)
{Hooks::run('footer_load_lib')|noescape}

// In PHP code
Hooks::run('post_submit_add_action', $_POST);

docs.title: Attaching a Filter
docs.text:
Usage: Hooks::filter(string $hook_name, mixed $value);

The attached function must accept the value and return a (modified) value.

// Attach a filter to modify post titles
Hooks::attach('post_title_filter', function($title) {
    return strtoupper($title); // uppercase all titles
});

docs.title: Applying a Filter
docs.text:
// In PHP
$title = Hooks::filter('post_title_filter', $post->title);

// In a Latte template
{Hooks::filter('post_title_filter', $post->title)|noescape}

docs.title: Action Hooks
docs.text: Hook Trigger Point $data Type footer_load_lib Just before </body> in every page (none) header_load_meta Inside <head> of every page (none) post_submit_add_action After a new post is saved $_POST array post_sqldel_action After a post is deleted from database int $id admin_page_dashboard_action On the admin dashboard $data array admin_footer_action At the bottom of every admin page (none) user_login_action After a successful user login $data array user_register_action After a new user registers $data array

docs.title: Filter Hooks
docs.text: Hook What It Filters post_title_filter Post title string before display post_submit_title_filter Title string during save (sanitize) post_submit_content_filter Content string during save (sanitize) post_content_filter Post body HTML before render system_security_headers_args CSP rules array (directives and sources)

docs.title: Priority
docs.text:
Attach multiple callbacks to the same hook. Lower priority numbers run first.

Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['Analytics', 'load'], 5);  // runs first
Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['Ads', 'load'], 20);        // runs later

docs.title: Registering Hooks in a Theme
docs.text:
Put all hook registrations inside the theme class constructor in function.php:

class MyTheme
{
    public function __construct()
    {
        Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['MyTheme', 'loadJs']);
        Hooks::attach('header_load_meta', ['MyTheme', 'loadCss']);
        Hooks::attach('post_title_filter', ['MyTheme', 'addIcon']);
    }

    public static function loadJs()
    {
        echo '<script src="' . Url::theme() . '/js/app.js"></script>';
    }

    public static function loadCss()
    {
        echo '<link rel="stylesheet" href="' . Url::theme() . '/css/style.css">';
    }

    public static function addIcon($title)
    {
        return '📖 ' . $title;
    }
}
new MyTheme();

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hooks Class API — Full method reference.
  • Create a Theme — Where to register hooks in a theme.
  • Create a Module — Using hooks in modules.

docs.title: Using UiBuilder & OptionsBuilder
docs.text: This guide provides a comprehensive overview of the two primary layout engines in GeniXCMS v2.0+: UiBuilder and OptionsBuilder. These tools are designed to standardize the administrative interface into a modern, compact, "industrial ERP" aesthetic.

docs.title: 1. OptionsBuilder
docs.text: OptionsBuilder is a specialized engine for creating professional theme and module options panels. It features a fixed three-section layout: Topbar (for saving), Sidebar (for tab navigation), and Main Content (for settings).

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Fluent Interface: Designed for rapid configuration of complex settings.
  • Built-in Typography Group: Handles font-family, size, weight, and color in a single row.
  • Color Presets: Support for quick style presets that users can apply with one click.
  • Frontend CSS Generator: Automatically generates responsive CSS and Google Font imports based on saved settings.

docs.title: Schema Definition
docs.text:
An OptionsBuilder schema is an array of "tabs". Each tab contains "cards" or "sections".

$schema = [
    [
        'id'    => 'general',
        'label' => 'General Settings',
        'icon'  => 'bi bi-gear',
        'group' => 'Core', // Sidebar grouping
        'cards' => [
            [
                'title' => 'Branding',
                'cols'  => 2, // Grid columns
                'fields' => [
                    ['type' => 'text', 'name' => 'site_name', 'label' => 'Site Name'],
                    ['type' => 'color', 'name' => 'primary_color', 'label' => 'Primary Accent']
                ]
            ]
        ]
    ]
];

docs.title: Implementation Pattern
docs.text:
$opt = Options::get_all(); // Get all settings
$builder = new OptionsBuilder($opt);
$builder->render($schema);

docs.title: 2. UiBuilder
docs.text: UiBuilder is a generic, highly flexible engine for creating module pages, dashboards, and CRUD forms. Unlike OptionsBuilder, it is non-opinionated about the overall page structure and can be integrated into any gxadmin view.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Dynamic Header: Supports sticky headers with breadcrumbs and action buttons.
  • Nested Grid System: Build complex layouts using rows and columns.
  • Diverse Elements: Support for stats cards, alerts, switch toggles, raw HTML, and data tables.
  • Modern Aesthetic: Hardcoded to use Bootstrap 5 + GeniXCMS ERP styles.

docs.title: Schema Structure
docs.text: UiBuilder uses a hierarchical schema: 1. Header: Page identity and global actions. 2. Tabs (Optional): Navigation within the page. 3. Content: A list of row or card elements.

docs.title: Example: Media Settings Schema
docs.text:
$schema = [
    'header' => [
        'title' => 'Media Library',
        'button' => [
            'type' => 'button', 'name' => 'change', 'label' => 'Save', 
            'icon' => 'bi bi-save', 'class' => 'btn btn-primary'
        ]
    ],
    'content' => [
        ['type' => 'row', 'items' => [
            ['width' => 8, 'content' => [
                ['type' => 'card', 'title' => 'Configuration', 'body_elements' => [
                    ['type' => 'input', 'name' => 'path', 'label' => 'Storage Path']
                ]]
            ]],
            ['width' => 4, 'content' => [
                ['type' => 'alert', 'style' => 'info', 'content' => 'Tip: Use WebP for speed.']
            ]]
        ]]
    ]
];

docs.title: Field Types Comparison
docs.text: Field Type OptionsBuilder UiBuilder Description text / input ✅ ✅ Standard text input. number ✅ ✅ Numeric input with min/max. select ✅ ✅ Dropdown menu. toggle / switch ✅ ✅ Boolean on/off switch. color ✅ ✅ Hex color picker + text input. range ✅ ❌ Slider with unit badge. typo_row ✅ ❌ Multi-field typography row. stats_card ❌ ✅ Dashboard-style metric display. tab_mode sidebar js / link Navigation logic.

docs.title: 1. Centralized Notifications
docs.text:
Always include the notification hook before rendering the form: 

echo Hooks::run('admin_page_notif_action', $data);

docs.title: 2. Form Submission
docs.text:
Wrap UiBuilder in a form and include the security token: 

echo '<form method="POST">';
$builder = new UiBuilder($schema);
$builder->render();
echo '<input type="hidden" name="token" value="'.TOKEN.'">';
echo '</form>';

docs.title: 3. Sticky Headers
docs.text: UiBuilder headers are sticky by default. Ensure enough bottom padding in your content to prevent the footer from overlapping or content from being hidden behind the header on scroll.

docs.title: 4. Naming Convention
docs.text:
• Use Snake Case for OptionsBuilder field names (e.g., theme_primary_color).
  • Use Explicit Names for UiBuilder buttons to avoid collisions (e.g., 'name' => 'save_mod_settings').

docs.title: 1. Typography Prefix Mapping
docs.text: When using typo_row in OptionsBuilder, ensure you use these standard prefixes to leverage the automatic CSS generation engine: - typo_body: Controls main document flow and paragraphs. - typo_nav: Targets the navigation bar and primary link structures. - typo_brand: Specifically for site-wide logo/identity typography. - typo_h1 - typo_h6: Granular control over the page's heading hierarchy. - typo_meta: For article data like dates, author names, and taxonomies. - typo_list: Standardizes unordered and ordered list presentations. - typo_blockquote: Controls stylistic citations and blockquote elements. - typo_pagination: For UI navigation buttons in multi-page indexes.

docs.title: 2. Overriding Framework Styles
docs.text:
You can pass theme or module-specific overrides using the extraCssRules parameter in the OptionsBuilder::generateFrontendCSS configuration array: 

{OptionsBuilder::generateFrontendCSS($opt, [
    'extraCssRules' => '.my-special-selector { color: var(--primary-color) !important; }'
])|noescape}

docs.title: 3. Global Notification & Toast System
docs.text: GeniXCMS v2.0+ utilizes a unified AJAX notification system provided by OptionsBuilder: - ID Requirement: Ensure your success/error elements use #gx-toast-container for the wrapper and .gx-toast for individual messages. - Auto-Extraction: The framework's AJAX handler automatically detects and displays these notifications from any theme's options.php response.

docs.title: 4. Instance vs Static Calls
docs.text:
• Use new OptionsBuilder($opt) for rendering the admin panel.
  • Use OptionsBuilder::renderCSS() (static) to inject admin-side styles.
  • Use OptionsBuilder::generateFrontendCSS() (static) in your theme's frontend header.

docs.title: Using Vite for Theme Asset Bundling
docs.text: GeniXCMS 2.0.0-alpha introduces native support for Vite. This allows you to use SCSS, Tailwind CSS, TypeScript, and modern JavaScript with Hot Module Replacement (HMR) during development.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Prerequisites
  • Project Structure
  • Vite Configuration
  • Integration in Theme
  • Development Workflow
  • Production Build

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Node.js (v18+)
  • NPM or PNPM
  • Vite installed in your theme directory.

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
A typical Vite-powered GeniXCMS theme looks like this:

inc/themes/my-modern-theme/
├── assets/
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── main.js        ← Entry Point
│   │   └── style.scss     ← Your Styles
│   └── dist/              ← Build output (auto-generated)
├── index.php
├── package.json
└── vite.config.js

docs.title: Vite Configuration (vite.config.js)
docs.text:
You need to configure Vite to work with the CMS. Example:

import { defineConfig } from 'vite';

export default defineConfig({
  build: {
    manifest: true,
    outDir: 'assets/dist',
    rollupOptions: {
      input: 'assets/src/main.js',
    },
  },
  server: {
    cors: true,
    strictPort: true,
    port: 5173,
    hmr: {
        host: 'localhost',
    }
  },
});

docs.title: Integration in Theme
docs.text:
In your theme's header.php, use the Vite::client() helper to inject assets:

<!-- Auto-inject Vite client or production bundle -->
<?= Vite::client('assets/src/main.js'); ?>

[!NOTE] The helper will automatically use the HMR client if Dev Mode is enabled in settings, otherwise it will load the hashed files from assets/dist/manifest.json.

docs.title: Development Workflow
docs.text:
1. Enable Hot Reloading in Settings > Assets.
  2. Run Vite dev server in your theme folder: 
    npm install
npm run dev
  3. Edit your SCSS/JS files. Changes will reflect instantly in the browser without page reload.

docs.title: Production Build
docs.text:
When you're ready to deploy:

  1. Disable Hot Reloading in settings.
  2. Run the build command: 
    npm run build
  3. Vite will generate a manifest.json in assets/dist/. GeniXCMS will automatically read this file to load the correct versioned assets.

[!TIP] You can also use Tailwind CSS by just adding tailwind.config.js and importing it in your style.scss. Vite will handle the rest!

docs.title: Categories
docs.text:
Posts need Category. Before creating a new post, it's better if we add new Category first. At least One Category.

Click the Category Manager at the left menu.

On the top right, there is a button Add Category, click it and a modal window will appear.

Choose the parent category if any. If no category exist just left it blank.

Fill in the name of the category, after all is set, click Save Changes button.

After category was added, will appear a box with category name.

At the images, it shows two name. The top is Main Category, and The second is Sub Category.

Apparently, the main category cannot edited yet.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
After You logged in to the Administrator area. Now it's time to Setting Up your site.

Click on the Setting Menu on the left side. A new page will shows up .

docs.title: General Settings
docs.text:
There are some input fields appear. Fill in the necessary field. Especially :

  • Website Slogan
  • Website Keywords
  • Website Description

Those are important. Especially for SEO.

After those are filled up, go to the next tab.

docs.title: E-Mail
docs.text: This page is for email sending features. There are some options to be picked.

docs.title: PHP Mail
docs.text: If you want to send email just using your mail program just pick Mail at the Mail/SMTP options. And the other field is not necessary.

docs.title: SMTP Server
docs.text:
If You want to use outgoing email using SMTP so pick SMTP at the Mail/SMTP options. And fill in other input.

Choose whether the SMTP is using Plain Authentification or Use SSL.

Fill the SMTP Mailserver, SMTP Username, and SMTP Password you had.

docs.title: Social
docs.text: This is Optional. Depends on the Themes you are using. If no options of using this so left it blank.

docs.title: Logo
docs.text:
Now it's time to personalize your site. Upload Your own Logo. Choose a small images please, so it won't make your site bloating. You can choose wheter Upload it by your self or just use already online Logo.

If you want to use already uploaded logo at the internet, just thick the checkbox button at the Use Image URL and paste the image address.

It automatically will use the image url rather than the Uploaded Logo.

docs.title: Favicon
docs.text: Insert your favicon url. Full url if i may say. Since it won't trouble when the SMART_URL is activated.

docs.title: Library
docs.text:
These are the dependencies we had to load for website to work well. We need :

  • jQuery
  • Fontawesome
  • Bootstrap
  • Summernote Editor
  • Bootstrap Validator

jQuery and Fontawesome are from CDN and others are at assets directory.

For jQuery, You can input which version you wanto to use. Just fill in the version number. Others are still not supported yet.

docs.title: Posts
docs.text:
Post Per Page is the Options how many posts are appear at the front page.

Pinger is an address of Pinger for search engine. We already insert some. Just add it if you want.

Pagination type, there are two options. First is Number, and second is Pager. Number means the page will shows as numbers. and pager the page will shows as Prev and Next.

docs.title: Payment
docs.text:
Currently We are still in developing the Payment Class. Especially the PayPal Class.

Even that, this field is functional. And can be run, see our sample store SerieShop2

docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text: The Dashboard is the first page you see after logging into the admin area. It provides an at-a-glance overview of your site's content and activity.

docs.title: Accessing the Dashboard
docs.text: Navigate to your admin URL (e.g., https://yoursite.com/admin/) and log in with your administrator credentials. The dashboard loads automatically.

docs.title: Statistics Panel
docs.text:
The top of the dashboard displays key content counters:

Metric Description Total Posts Number of published blog posts Total Pages Number of static pages Total Comments Comments pending and approved Total Users Registered user accounts

docs.title: Recent Posts
docs.text: A quick-access list of the most recently published posts, with direct Edit links.

docs.title: System Info
docs.text: Displays the current GeniXCMS version and PHP environment information.

docs.title: Admin Navigation
docs.text:
The left sidebar provides quick links to all management areas:

  • Posts — Create and manage blog articles
  • Pages — Manage static pages
  • Categories — Organize posts by category
  • Tags — Manage post tags
  • Comments — Moderate reader comments
  • Menus — Build your site navigation
  • Media — File manager and image library
  • Modules — Install and manage extensions
  • Appearance — Change themes and widget placement
  • Users — Manage user accounts and roles
  • Settings — Configure global site options

docs.title: Quick Tips
docs.text:
Tip

Use the Settings > General page to configure your site name, email, timezone, and SEO metadata before publishing content.

Info

Widget areas are managed under Appearance > Widgets. Drag and drop widgets to assign them to sidebar or footer positions.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Installing GeniXCMS is easy. Below are some step to follow to install GeniXCMS from zero.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Before starting installation, please make sure your webserver is meet this requirements.

  • Using PHP >= 8
  • Using MySQL Server at least version 4

That's the minimal requirements we need. But don't forget to install the webserver already. Since this application will run on the webserver. You can use any kinds webserver as you want.

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
• Nginx Server + PHP-FPM
  • MariaDB Server

docs.title: Uploading Files
docs.text:
Before we install the CMS, we had to put the files into the webserver. Depends on the hosting you are using, this is basically the same proccess to all hosting provider.

You can upload it via FTP, or File Managers on the Control Panel. Please Ask your hosting provider about how to do this.

docs.title: File Permission
docs.text:
The next step is setting up the file permission so it can be write during the installation proccess. Please set the permission of these files to 777 (writable).

  • inc/config
  • assets/images
  • assets/images/uploads
  • assets/images/uploads/thumbs
  • assets/media/audios
  • assets/media/images
  • assets/media/videos

docs.title: Preparing Database
docs.text:
Installation cannot be run before the database is set. So, go to the Database manager at your Hosting Control Panel and create new database, and assign the user to the database.

Save the database username, database name and database the password. We will need this for the next step.

docs.title: Run the installation
docs.text: To run the installer is simple. Just open your site at the browser. An installation wizard will shows up. Just fill in the field and follow the wizard until finish.

docs.title: Login Dashboard
docs.text:
After the installation is done. Now follow the link at the end of the installation and login to dashboard.

Login with Username and Password just You submit.

docs.title: Menus
docs.text:
Open the Menu manager at the left. Click the Menus link.

There are two menus already provided. So, You don't need to create a new one.

mainmenu and footer is the Menu ID. Use it to call the menu at the template.

Click one of the menu. It will shows list of menu items like this

docs.title: Add Item
docs.text:
To add a Menu item just click the Add Item tab, a new tab will appear.

To create a main menu, Left the Parent Menu blank. But if it's a submenu, choose the parent menu.

After that, Input the Menu Name. eg: About Us, Profile, etc..

Menu Class is used if your themes need the menu item had a specific css class. If not, left it blank.

docs.title: Menu Type
docs.text:
There are some menu types provided. Page, Categories, Mod, and Custom Link.

Pick one of them as you want. But don't forget to check the radio button.

docs.title: Modules
docs.text: The Modules system lets you extend GeniXCMS with additional functionality — from contact forms to galleries, newsletters, and custom integrations — without modifying the core CMS files.

docs.title: Managing Modules
docs.text: Navigate to Admin > Modules to see the list of all installed modules.

docs.title: Installing a Module
docs.text:
1. Place your module folder inside inc/mod/your-module-name/.
  2. Go to Admin > Modules.
  3. Your module will appear in the list. Click Install to activate it.

docs.title: Uninstalling a Module
docs.text: Click Uninstall next to the module. This deactivates the module but does not delete its files.

docs.title: Module Settings
docs.text: If a module provides an admin settings page (options.php), a Settings button will appear next to the module in the list.

docs.title: Bundled Modules
docs.text:
GeniXCMS includes several built-in modules:

Module Description Contact Form Adds a "Contact Us" page accessible via the menu manager Dynamic Builder Drag-and-drop page builder for creating custom layouts Newsletter Email newsletter subscription form Code Highlight Syntax highlighting for code blocks in posts Classic WYSIWYG Alternative rich text editor (TinyMCE) SSO Auth Single Sign-On authentication integration Books Book listing and review module Infograph Data visualization / chart integration SEOnix Advanced on-page SEO tools

docs.title: Adding Module Pages to Navigation
docs.text:
Some modules add frontend pages (e.g., a contact form page). To link to them:

  1. Go to Admin > Menus.
  2. Select your menu and click Add Item.
  3. Choose Mod as the menu type.
  4. Select the module's page from the dropdown.
  5. Set a label and save.

docs.title: Building a Custom Module
docs.text: See the How to Create a Module guide for step-by-step instructions on building your own extension.

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
On the left Menu, Click the Pages menu. A new page will appear with empty list.

To make a new pages. Just click at the top right button named Add New. A new page with a form will shows.

Insert the Post title, Post content, and Publish it.

There are two options for page, Publish status and Dates. You can modify it as you like.

After a post is submited back to the pages manager and see if the page is submitted succesfully.

To edit or delete the page, there are some options at the right of the page. Edit, Delete and If you want to modify it massively, jusst check the checkbox and click the submit button.

docs.title: Posts
docs.text:
On the left Menu, Click the Posts menu. A new page will appear with empty list.

To make a new post. Just click at the top right button named Add New. A new page with a form will shows.

Insert the Post title, Post content, Pick a Category and Publish it.

There are some options, Category, Publish status and Dates. You can modify it as you like.

After a post is submited back to the posts manager and see if the post is submitted succesfully.

To edit or delete the post, there are some options at the right of the post. Edit, Delete and If you want to modify it massively, jusst check the checkbox and click the submit button.

docs.title: Users
docs.text: This is the dashboard for User Manager. In here will shows list of users with two group, as Administrator and General Members.

docs.title: Creating User
docs.text:
Creating a user is just one step. Click on the Add User button.

A new modal windows will appear like this .

Fill in all the field, choose the group level and click Save Changes.

docs.title: Editing User
docs.text:
To edit the user, choose one of the user and click the edit button at the right.

A new page will shows up with the Edit User form on it.

Change the field as you desired and click Update on the top right.

docs.title: Deleting User
docs.text: To Delete a user, just choose the user and click the Delete button on the right.
